html:
Re-Roofing Retail and Mill-Adjacent Buildings Along US-176 in Inman

Inman grew up around its textile mills, and the roof stock along US-176 today reflects both eras: retail storefronts and service buildings built in the last thirty years, and older mill-adjacent warehouse and shop space that predates most current code requirements. We treat the two categories differently once we're standing on the roof, because the code compliance questions aren't the same.

Strip Retail Roofs on US-176

Retail buildings along the highway are mostly low-slope construction with membrane or built-up roofing over a shared or individually parceled structure. Multi-tenant retail buildings raise a coordination question we deal with regularly: a roof leak over one unit can originate from a drain or seam serving the whole building, which means the repair scope and the cost allocation have to be worked out with the property owner or manager before work starts, not after.

Where a retail roof is nearing the end of its service life, we look at whether a full-building replacement makes more sense than patching individual tenant spaces piecemeal, since patchwork repairs on an aging membrane tend to generate repeat service calls.

Older Mill-Adjacent Industrial Buildings

Buildings that grew up alongside Inman's mill operations often have wood or steel truss roof framing with built-up or modified bitumen roofing added or replaced multiple times since original construction. When we re-roof one of these buildings, the permit process typically requires bringing the assembly up to current code, including insulation R-value and wind uplift resistance, even if the building itself is legacy construction. We flag that requirement up front so it doesn't surprise an owner mid-project.

Code Upgrades Triggered by Re-Roofing

South Carolina's energy code sets minimum insulation requirements for commercial roof replacement, and a re-roof permit generally triggers compliance with current standards regardless of what was there before. On older buildings this can mean adding insulation thickness beyond what the original roof carried, which affects both material selection and how flashing heights at parapets and curbs need to be adjusted.

We submit the insulation calculation and product data sheets with the permit application so the reviewing office can confirm compliance without a back-and-forth request for missing information, which keeps a project on schedule once tear-off begins.

Weather Exposure Along the Corridor

This part of Spartanburg County sees the same severe thunderstorm and hail pattern common across the Upstate, with wind-driven rain during summer storm cells testing seam and flashing integrity on flat roofs. We inspect flashing at parapet walls and rooftop penetrations closely after major storm events, since wind-driven water intrusion at those details is more common than membrane field failure on a properly installed roof.

Hail is also a recurring concern for the built-up and modified bitumen roofs common on the older mill-adjacent buildings, since granule loss from hail impact can accelerate UV degradation of the underlying membrane even when no immediate leak develops. We check granule wear patterns as part of any post-storm inspection.

Service Business Roofs Along the Corridor

US-176 also carries a number of auto service, equipment rental, and light commercial service businesses whose buildings combine a retail front with a shop or bay area behind it, similar in some ways to the mixed-use structures found elsewhere in the county. These buildings often have rooftop exhaust or ventilation equipment tied to shop operations, and we pay particular attention to penetration flashing around that equipment, since it tends to see more thermal cycling than a typical HVAC curb.

Where a shop building has an oil separator or paint booth exhaust venting through the roof, we use penetration seal materials rated for the specific chemical exposure involved rather than a standard pitch pocket detail, since generic sealants can break down faster under that kind of exposure than they would around a normal HVAC curb.

What We Check Before Quoting Re-Roof Work

  • Current roof assembly type, age, and layer count
  • Deck condition and moisture presence via core sample or scan
  • Code-required insulation R-value for the current permit cycle
  • Flashing height requirements at parapets and curbs for the new assembly thickness
  • Drainage adequacy for the building's actual roof plan
  • Multi-tenant coordination needs where applicable

That information shapes both the proposal and the permit drawings we prepare for submission.

Questions From Inman Building Owners

Does re-roofing an older building require bringing it up to current insulation code?

Generally yes. A re-roof permit typically requires the new assembly to meet current energy code insulation requirements even on legacy construction.

Who is responsible for roof repairs on a multi-tenant retail building?

That depends on the lease structure and how the roof was originally built. We document our findings clearly so ownership can determine responsibility, and we can work directly with a property manager on cost allocation.

How do you handle a leak that could be coming from a shared roof drain?

We trace the water path before assuming the source, since a leak inside one tenant space can originate from a drain, seam, or penetration serving a different part of the building.

Does adding insulation during a re-roof change the parapet flashing?

Yes. Increased insulation thickness raises the roof surface relative to the parapet, so flashing height and termination bar placement usually need to be adjusted to maintain proper coverage.

What storm damage do you see most often on Inman roofs?

Wind-driven rain intrusion at parapet flashing and penetration details after summer thunderstorms is more common here than membrane field failure on a properly maintained roof.
